summary: Adam presents a fascinating contradiction: a self-described 'saver' who spends over €100 weekly on food, primarily through delivery apps. His extreme commute situation (4-5 hours daily from rural Cavan to Dublin) has created a lifestyle entirely dependent on delivery services during weekdays, when he crashes on a friend's couch. He's developed sophisticated platform strategies, using Uber for chain restaurants due to better promotions and Deliveroo for independent restaurants with better selection. Despite claiming to enjoy cooking and being 'really picky on money,' his actual behavior reveals someone who has essentially outsourced meal planning to delivery apps out of necessity. His guilt around tipping drivers and not going out physically suggests awareness that his delivery dependence isn't entirely by choice, but rather a consequence of his challenging living situation. Adam represents the 'convenience-dependent' student archetype - someone whose circumstances have made delivery not a luxury but a practical necessity, yet who maintains the psychological framework of being budget-conscious even while spending heavily on convenience.
personality_notes: Articulate and self-aware, with a tendency toward self-contradiction. Presents himself as money-conscious while demonstrating high spending. Shows genuine enthusiasm when discussing food and cooking, but pragmatic acceptance of his delivery-dependent reality.
food_relationship: Food delivery has become his primary meal solution due to extreme commuting, creating dependence masked as convenience choice.

key_moments.description: Reveals spending €100+ weekly on food, 80% on takeaways, despite claiming to be 'really picky on money'
key_moments.significance: Core contradiction between self-perception as saver and actual high-spending behavior on convenience

key_moments.description: Expresses guilt about not tipping drivers and staying home instead of going out physically
key_moments.significance: Shows internal conflict about delivery dependence and awareness it's not entirely voluntary

key_moments.description: Reveals he tried being a delivery rider but quit due to lack of orders
key_moments.significance: Unique perspective having been on both sides of the delivery ecosystem

what_not_said: Financial stress or concern about his high food spending
Social isolation from his unusual living arrangement
Academic performance impact from extreme commuting
Long-term sustainability of his current lifestyle
initial_hypotheses: His extreme commute has created artificial delivery dependence that he rationalizes as choice
Price sensitivity is performative - he's actually willing to pay premium for convenience
His rider experience may make him more empathetic to delivery workers but also more strategic about platform choice
Living situation instability drives food behavior more than student budget constraints
questions_for_coding: How do extreme commuting students rationalize high delivery spending?
What role does temporary accommodation play in food delivery dependence?
How does prior gig work experience influence platform loyalty and usage patterns?
What's the relationship between stated money consciousness and actual spending behavior?
